text: A seventy-four-year-old woman who underwent per-oral endoscopic myotomy for achalasia presented three months later with resolved dysphagia but increasing reflux refractory to PPI therapy. Upper GI demonstrated the presence of moderate spontaneous gastroesophageal reflux above the level of the aortic arch. Partial fundoplication was recommended to the patient.

The surgeon uses an optical five millimeter port to enter the insufflated abdomen, placing two additional five millimeter and two ten millimeter ports in an M configuration in the upper abdomen. A flexible retractor elevates the left lateral segment of the liver, revealing no visible adhesions or scarring in the region of the prior myotomy.
An upper endoscopy reveals the gastroesophageal junction to be wide open, with no visible signs of esophagitis.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to open the gastrohepatic ligament and the phrenoesophageal ligament in two layers, carefully preserving the hepatic branch of the vagus nerve.
The surgeon incises the peritoneum along the right and left crura, circumferentially mobilizing the esophagus to prepare for fundoplication.

Minimal dissection is performed along the anterior surface of the esophagus, with no visible scarring from the prior myotomy. The surgeon enters the lesser sac along the greater curvature of the stomach, inferior to the spleen's lower pole. The short gastric vessels are divided using a harmonic scalpel, and the posterior aspect of the stomach fundus is fully mobilized.
The surgeon identifies both vagus nerves and encircles the esophagus with a vessel loop, ensuring clear visualization and control of the esophagus during the procedure.

The surgeon completes the crural dissection and passes the posterior aspect of the upper fundus of the stomach behind the distal esophagus. The anterior and posterior vagus nerves are verified in position. A Toupee fundoplication is constructed using three rows of interrupted 2-0 silk sutures, with superior sutures placed at the ten o'clock and two o'clock positions, securing the muscular wall of the esophagus, the fundus of the stomach, and the diaphragm.
The surgeon places two additional interrupted sutures on either side, securing the muscular wall of the esophagus to the fundus of the stomach, completing the toupee fundoplication.
The surgeon secures the posterior aspect of the fundus to the crura using two 2-0 silk sutures. The vessel loop encircling the esophagus is removed prior to completing the fundoplication.

At the completion of the fundoplication, the surgeon observes no tension in the surgical site. An upper endoscopy is performed, revealing a newly formed posterior antireflux valve around the lower esophageal sphincter, with no obstruction at the gastroesophageal junction.
